subject: Biology
text: In multicellular animals, a group of similar cells along with intercellular substances perform a specific function. Such an organisation is called tissue. All complex animals consist of only four basic types of tissues. These tissues are organised in specific proportion and pattern to form an organ like stomach, lung, heart and kidney. When two or more organs perform a common function by their physical and/or chemical interaction, they together form an organ system, e.g., digestive system, respiratory system, etc. Cells, tissues, organs and organ systems split up the work in a way that exhibits division of labour and contribute to the survival of the body as a whole. The basic tissues organise to form organs which in turn associate to form organ systems in the multicellular organisms. Such an organisation is essential for more efficient and better coordinated activities of millions of cells constituting an organism. Each organ in our body is made of one or more type of tissues. For example, our heart consists of all the four types of tissues, i.e., epithelial, connective, muscular and neural. The complexity in organ and organ systems displays an evolutionary trend. Morphology refers to the study of form or externally visible features, while anatomy conventionally is used for the study of the morphology of internal organs.

subject: Biology
text: Frogs can live both on land and in freshwater and belong to class Amphibia of phylum Chordata. The most common species of frog found in India is Rana tigrina. They are cold blooded or poikilotherms, meaning their body temperature varies with the temperature of the environment. Frogs have the ability to change their colour to hide from enemies, a protective coloration called mimicry or camouflage. During peak summer and winter, they take shelter in deep burrows to protect themselves from extreme heat and cold, known as aestivation (summer sleep) and hibernation (winter sleep). The skin of a frog is smooth and slippery due to the presence of mucus and is always maintained in a moist condition. The colour of the dorsal side of the body is generally olive green with dark irregular spots, while the ventral side is uniformly pale yellow. The frog never drinks water but absorbs it through the skin. The body is divisible into head and trunk, while a neck and tail are absent. Above the mouth, a pair of nostrils is present. Eyes are bulged and covered by a nictitating membrane that protects them while in water. On either side of the eyes, a membranous tympanum receives sound signals.

subject: Biology
text: The forelimbs and hind limbs of the frog help in swimming, walking, leaping and burrowing. The hind limbs end in five digits and are larger and muscular than the fore limbs, which end in four digits. Feet have webbed digits that help in swimming. Frogs exhibit sexual dimorphism; males are distinguished by sound-producing vocal sacs and a copulatory pad on the first digit of the fore limbs. The body cavity accommodates organ systems like digestive, circulatory, respiratory, nervous, excretory and reproductive systems. The digestive system consists of an alimentary canal and digestive glands. The alimentary canal is short because frogs are carnivores, resulting in a reduced intestinal length. The mouth opens into the buccal cavity, leading to the oesophagus through the pharynx. The oesophagus is a short tube opening into the stomach, which continues as the intestine, rectum and opens outside by the cloaca. Liver secretes bile stored in the gall bladder, while the pancreas produces pancreatic juice. Food is captured by a bilobed tongue and digested by HCl and gastric juices in the stomach. Partially digested food, called chyme, passes to the duodenum, which receives bile and pancreatic juices. Digested food is absorbed by villi and microvilli in the intestine.

subject: Biology
text: Frogs respire on land and in water by different methods. In water, the skin acts as an aquatic respiratory organ via cutaneous respiration, where dissolved oxygen is exchanged by diffusion. On land, the buccal cavity, skin and lungs act as respiratory organs. Respiration by lungs is pulmonary respiration. Lungs are a pair of elongated, pink sac-like structures in the upper trunk. During aestivation and hibernation, gaseous exchange takes place through the skin. The vascular system is a well-developed closed type, including a lymphatic system. The blood vascular system involves the heart, blood vessels and blood. The heart is a muscular three-chambered structure (two atria and one ventricle) covered by a pericardium. A triangular structure called sinus venosus joins the right atrium, receiving blood through the major veins called vena cava. The ventricle opens into a sac-like conus arteriosus on the ventral side of the heart. Blood is carried by arteries and collected by veins. Special venous connections called the hepatic portal system (between liver and intestine) and renal portal system (between kidney and lower body) are present. Blood consists of plasma and nucleated RBCs containing haemoglobin, WBCs and platelets. Lymph differs from blood by lacking certain proteins and RBCs.

subject: Biology
text: The elimination of nitrogenous wastes is carried out by a well-developed excretory system consisting of a pair of kidneys, ureters, cloaca and urinary bladder. The kidneys are compact, dark red, bean-like structures situated posteriorly in the body cavity. Each kidney is composed of functional units called nephrons. In males, ureters act as urinogenital ducts opening into the cloaca, while in females, ureters and oviducts open separately. The frog is a ureotelic animal, excreting urea. The system for control and coordination includes the neural system and endocrine glands. Prominent endocrine glands include the pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, pineal body, pancreatic islets, adrenals and gonads. The nervous system is organised into central (brain and spinal cord), peripheral (cranial and spinal nerves) and autonomic systems. There are ten pairs of cranial nerves. The brain is enclosed in a cranium and divided into fore-brain, mid-brain and hind-brain. The fore-brain includes olfactory lobes, paired cerebral hemispheres and an unpaired diencephalon. The mid-brain has optic lobes. The hind-brain consists of the cerebellum and medulla oblongata, the latter continuing into the spinal cord. Sense organs include sensory papillae (touch), taste buds, nasal epithelium (smell), simple eyes in orbits and tympanum with internal ears (hearing and equilibrium).

subject: Biology
text: Frogs have well-organised reproductive systems. Male organs consist of a pair of yellowish ovoid testes adhered to kidneys by a double fold of peritoneum called mesorchium. Vasa efferentia (10-12) enter kidneys and open into Bidder’s canal, finally communicating with the urinogenital duct that opens into the cloaca. The female system includes a pair of ovaries situated near kidneys with no functional connection to them. A pair of oviducts opens separately into the cloaca. A mature female can lay 2500 to 3000 ova at a time. Fertilisation is external and takes place in water. Development involves a larval stage called a tadpole, which undergoes metamorphosis to become an adult. Frogs are beneficial for mankind as they eat insects, protecting crops. They maintain ecological balance as an important link in the food chain and food web. In some countries, the muscular legs of frogs are used as a food source by humans. This concludes the structural and functional overview of the frog as a representative vertebrate.
